lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: “They met me in the day of success: and I have learned

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: by the perfectest report, they have more in them than mortal knowledge.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: When I burned in desire to question them further, they made themselves

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: air, into which they vanished. Whiles I stood rapt in the wonder

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: of it, came missives from the king, who all-hailed me ‘Thane of

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Cawdor;’ by which title, before, these weird sisters saluted me, and referred

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: me to the coming on of time, with ‘Hail, king that shalt

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: be!’ This have I thought good to deliver thee, my dearest partner

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: of greatness, that thou mightst not lose the dues of rejoicing, by

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: being ignorant of what greatness is promised thee. Lay it to thy

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: heart, and farewell.” Glamis thou art, and Cawdor; and shalt be What

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: thou art promised: yet do I fear thy nature; It is too

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: full o’ the milk of human kindness To catch the nearest way:

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: thou wouldst be great; Art not without ambition, but without The illness

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: should attend it: what thou wouldst highly, That wouldst thou holily; wouldst

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: not play false, And yet wouldst wrongly win: thou’ldst have, great Glamis,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: That which cries “Thus thou must do, if thou have it; And

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: that which rather thou dost fear to do Than wishest should be

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: undone.” Hie thee hither, That I may pour my spirits in thine

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: ear; And chastise with the valour of my tongue All that impedes

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: thee from the golden round, Which fate and metaphysical aid doth seem

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: To have thee crown’d withal.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The king comes here to-night.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Thou’rt mad to say it:

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Is not thy master with him? who, were’t so,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Would have inform’d for preparation.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: So please you, it is true: our thane is coming:

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: One of my fellows had the speed of him,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Who, almost dead for breath, had scarcely more

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Than would make up his message.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Give him tending;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: He brings great news.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: The raven himself is hoarse

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: That croaks the fatal entrance of Duncan

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Under my battlements. Come, you spirits

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: That tend on mortal thoughts, unsex me here,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: And fill me from the crown to the toe top-full

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Of direst cruelty! make thick my blood;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Stop up the access and passage to remorse,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: That no compunctious visitings of nature

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Shake my fell purpose, nor keep peace between

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: The effect and it! Come to my woman’s breasts,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: And take my milk for gall, you murdering ministers,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Wherever in your sightless substances

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: You wait on nature’s mischief! Come, thick night,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: And pall thee in the dunnest smoke of hell,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: That my keen knife see not the wound it makes,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Nor heaven peep through the blanket of the dark,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: To cry “Hold, hold!”

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: My dearest love,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Duncan comes here to-night.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: And when goes hence?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To-morrow, as he purposes.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: O, never

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Shall sun that morrow see!

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Your face, my thane, is as a book where men

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: May read strange matters. To beguile the time,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Look like the time; bear welcome in your eye,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Your hand, your tongue: look like the innocent flower,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: But be the serpent under’t. He that’s coming

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Must be provided for: and you shall put

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: This night’s great business into my dispatch;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Which shall to all our nights and days to come

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Give solely sovereign sway and masterdom.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: We will speak further.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Only look up clear;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: To alter favour ever is to fear:

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Leave all the rest to me.

stage_directions.text: Enter Lady Macbeth, reading a letter.
Great Glamis! worthy Cawdor! Greater than both, by the all-hail hereafter! Thy letters have transported me beyond This ignorant present, and I feel now The future in the instant.

glosses.definition: the most reliable information
human understanding
messengers
the future that is approaching
report to you
Lady Macbeth's famous phrase: the gentle, nurturing compassion that makes one human — and that she thinks disqualifies Macbeth from murder
in a holy, scrupulous manner
supernatural help — 'metaphysical' here means 'beyond the physical,' NOT the modern philosophical sense
supernatural; beyond the physical — 'metaphysical' in the Elizabethan sense, NOT today's philosophical usage
bird of ill omen, announcing death
strip me of my femininity — Lady Macbeth's plea to be made capable of murder; the speech imagines womanhood itself as a barrier to cruelty

glosses.phrase: Stop up the access and passage to remorse
glosses.definition: block every channel through which conscience might enter
natural stirrings of remorse or pity — the instinctive human recoil from cruelty
of pity, conscience, or remorse
cruel, deadly intent
cruel; deadly
replace my mother's milk with bile — reversing nurture into poison
invisible beings; unseen spirits that attend on murder
attend on natural disasters or moral wrongs
darkest smoke of hell — Lady M's prayer for concealing darkness
cover yourself; dress yourself as if in a funeral shroud
the covering of darkness — the night figured as a bed-blanket hiding sin
deceive everyone looking; make the hours pass without suspicion
Lady Macbeth's key instruction to Macbeth on deception — look harmless, be poisonous
absolute royal power

annotations.title: Milk of human kindness
annotations.body:
Lady Macbeth's first characterization of her husband is the play's most famous phrase about him: 'too full o' the milk of human kindness.' The metaphor is precise and insulting. 'Milk' is maternal nurture — the substance that makes human beings human. Her complaint is that Macbeth has too much of it: he has retained the infant's dependency on care, the compassion that prevents atrocity.

The metaphor's intimacy matters. She does not figure him as weak or cowardly; she figures him as nursed. And in the next twelve lines she will speak her own 'unsex me here' speech, in which she asks to have her own milk replaced with gall. The two speeches are paired: his nurture is the problem, and her self-cancellation is the solution.

Reading *Macbeth* alongside Janet Adelman's *Suffocating Mothers*, this is the play's founding metaphor: the household has too much mother-substance, and the tragedy will unfold as an attempt to purge it. The witches are mothers without children; the Macbeths are a marriage without visible children (L. C. Knights's famous essay question); and the murders are committed in rooms where breast-feeding ought to happen.
sources: L. C. Knights, 'How Many Children Had Lady Macbeth?' (1933)
Cleanth Brooks, 'The Naked Babe and the Cloak of Manliness' (1947)

annotations.title: 'Unsex me here': the speech and its failure
annotations.body:
Lady Macbeth's invocation — 'unsex me here,' 'take my milk for gall,' 'make thick my blood' — is the play's most famous request for self-transformation. It asks the 'spirits / That tend on mortal thoughts' to strip her of everything that makes her a woman as the period understood the category: compassion, nurture, the possibility of regret.

The speech succeeds theatrically and fails metaphysically. She gets what she asks for in the short term — the resolve to plan the murder, the cold practicality of 2.2 ('a little water clears us of this deed'). But the play grants her no lasting transformation. By 5.1 she is sleepwalking in a nightdress, rubbing her hands and smelling blood on them. The 'compunctious visitings of nature' she prayed to stop have returned, deferred but not denied.

Shakespeare is quietly refusing her request. The speech is the play's strongest argument that the moral architecture of a human being cannot be dismantled by incantation. You can postpone conscience. You cannot delete it.

annotations.title: 'Look like the innocent flower, / But be the serpent under't'
annotations.body:
Lady Macbeth's instruction to Macbeth — look innocent, be poisonous — is the play's second paradox, paired with the Witches' 'Fair is foul' from 1.1. The two paradoxes frame the tragedy: the supernatural has announced that appearances deceive, and the wife has now given the husband a practical manual for deception.

The flower/serpent image will echo through the play. In 3.2 Macbeth will tell Lady Macbeth 'we have scotched the snake, not killed it' — and by then they are the snake. The couple who weaponize deception will be destroyed by the equivocations of the Sisters, whose prophecies 'palter with us in a double sense' (5.8). What Lady Macbeth teaches Macbeth in this scene, the Sisters will do to them both.
sources: Harold Goddard, *The Meaning of Shakespeare*, vol. 2 (1951)

trials.prompt: Lady Macbeth calls Macbeth 'too full o' the milk of human kindness.' What is she accusing him of?
options: Retaining the compassionate, nurturing nature that prevents him from committing murder

trials.prompt: In her 'unsex me here' speech, Lady Macbeth is:
options: Invoking spirits to strip her of compassion and maternal instinct so she can commit murder

trials.prompt: What does the play do with Lady Macbeth's 'unsex me' request?
options: She gets short-term resolve, but 5.1's sleepwalking shows the transformation failed — conscience returns

trials.prompt: 'Look like the innocent flower, / But be the serpent under't' continues what earlier pattern?
options: The 'Fair is foul' paradox from 1.1 — deceptive appearance is now a practical instruction

trials.prompt: Why does Lady Macbeth learn Duncan will sleep at her castle?
options: A letter from Macbeth, followed by a messenger announcing Duncan's arrival
